steps.text: Use specially prepared canisters at least 6 liters in volume that are suitable for trace gas analysis of the target compounds.
Ensure canisters can withstand numerous cycles of evacuation to high vacuum of 0.0067 kPa and pressurization to 377 kPa.
Use canisters with valves designed specifically for trace level measurements.
Ensure wetted portions of the valve are constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless steel and that the valve seal surfaces are metal to metal.
Prepare working-level standards for calibrating the GC-MS by dilution of a higher concentration stock standard gas.
Ensure the dynamic dilution system includes calibrated electronic mass flow controllers (MFCs) for the diluent gas and each standard gas.
Ensure a humidifier is included for the diluent gas.
Ensure a manifold or mixing chamber is available for combining diluent and standard gases.
Produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
Construct connection tubing of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Construct mixing chambers or manifolds of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Recalibrate the gas dilution system once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty ≤ 0.25%.
Report the results of the calibration whenever the dilution system is used.
Record the average flow rate during this test.
Prior to and after each field sampling event, establish or verify the flow rate of the sampling apparatus.
Assemble an evacuated canister and the sampling device connected to a certified flow meter in the same manner used for the flow control verification test.
Open the evacuated canister, allow sufficient time for the system to stabilize, and record the flowrate upstream of the flow control device.
Calculate the average flowrate.
Adjust the controller and repeat the flow check if the flow rate has changed and is outside the desired range.
You must demonstrate the sampling device and sampling system are leak-free immediately before you begin sampling.
Install the sampling device on an evacuated canister equipped with a MFCD and tightly cap the inlet to the sampling device.
Open the canister valve fully, and then re-close the valve and observe the vacuum/pressure gauge for a minimum of 2 minutes.
Record the data and time of the leak check on the Field Data Page.
Conduct the sample bias test at least every six months, and after cleaning, replacement of components, or collection of potentially contaminating samples.

Provide humidified (> 40% RH) HCF zero air through the sampling device into the canister, and then analyze the sample according to Section 11 of this method and record the concentration measurement and maintain the results as part of a laboratory record associated with the sampling device.

Provide humidified (> 40% RH) reference standard air through the sampling device into a canister, then analyze the sample according to Section 11 of this method and record the concentration measurement and maintain the results as part of a laboratory record associated with the sampling device.

The results must show that the concentration of the target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample collected through the sampling unit is not greater than 20 pptv higher than the native concentration of the target compounds in the reference sample.
Take steps to remove the contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Repeat the analytical zero-air verification to ensure that you have mitigated any problems before using the analytical instrumentation.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation is non-biasing under known standards as part of an instrument’s annual calibration.
Analyze a humidified reference standard in duplicate containing all target compounds at approximately 100 to 500 pptv each.
Ensure that the target compounds in the sample collected through the sampling device are within ± 15% of the expected concentrations in the sample.
Consult the instrument if you identify problems related to poor response indicated by peak absence or minimal peak area.
Clean canisters and verify that the canisters meet cleanliness and vacuum criteria specified in Sections 8.3 through 8.5 of this method.
If canisters were previously cleaned and stored under pressure while awaiting use, evacuate the canisters prior to field deployment.
If canisters were stored under vacuum, verify that the canisters continue to meet vacuum threshold requirements.
Clean and verify the cleanliness and flowrates of sample devices that you will use for sampling, and ensure that a clean particulate filter is placed in the inlet of the sampling device.
Establish sample codes (unique identifiers) and develop field data page and/or chain of custody (COC)/sample collection data form(s).
If shipping equipment into the field, make sure you have the proper number of canisters and sampling devices for the number of samples required for the sampling location and QC samples.

Develop a unique sampling location ID that meets any requirements set in the applicable regulation and is in a secure location that protects the canister and sampling inlet from unwanted tampering or damage.
Document all activities associated with sampling on the field data page.
Verify that each canister has been cleaned and blanked within the last 30 days. Label each canister with a sample ID code and record the canister and sample ID on the field data page.

Verify the sample device is in working order and calibrate/verify the flow rate setting, if applicable, with a reference flow meter. Record the sample device ID, expected flowrate, and the reference flowrate if calibrated/verified in the field.

Attach the sampling device to the canister and locate at the appropriate sampling location. Record the sampling location ID, latitude, longitude, date, and time that you installed the canister on the field data page.

Measure and record the canister vacuum using the field pressure measurement gauge, and verify that the canister has not leaked and has sufficient vacuum to collect the sample. You must replace the canister if the initial pressure is not within -1 in. Hg absolute zero (-3.39 kPa or -0.5 psi).
Conduct leak checks as specified in Section 8.3.2 of this method and record the results on the field data page.

Open the canister valve. Record the date and time that you opened the valve as the start time, and record the initial canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page.
Sample for the period as defined in the applicable standard (e.g., 24 hours +/-).
At the end of the sampling period, close the valve. Record the date and time that you closed the valve as the end time.
Remove the sampling device and attach the field pressure measurement gauge.

Open the canister valve and measure and record the final canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page. Flag any canisters with a final pressure greater than -3 in. Hg gauge pressure (-10.2 kPag or -1.5 psig).
Disconnect the field canister pressure measurement gauge and replace with a cap.
If applicable, verify the sample device is still in working order and verify the flow rate setting with a reference flow meter. Record the final flowrate on the field data page.
Include the collection of a separate co-located sample for at least one sampling location using a discrete MFCD.

Collect canister field blanks. A canister blank is prepared by filling a canister with humidified clean diluent gas to approximately -15 in. Hg ± 1 in. Hg. Record the pressure and transport to the field site(s) to accompany field-collected canisters.
Analyze an IB at the beginning of the sequence and prior to analysis of the ICAL standard and daily CCV standard.
Analyze a laboratory MB prior to and following the ICAL in an ICAL sequence and prior to analyzing the CCV standard.
Analyze the canister field blank as part of the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field samples.
Analyze the CB when the ICAL is established and when preparing any new CCV standard.
Analyze the field duplicate during the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field sample.
Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to an evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as you add gases.
Add stock and diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Measure the canister pressure before and after standard and diluent gases are bled into the canister, and input these pressures into the calculation of the dilution factor and final concentrations.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.
Store standards prepared in canisters at ambient laboratory conditions in locations that are free of potential contaminants for up to 7 days.
Condition preconcentrator traps when first installed to eliminate contaminants that act as interferences or chromatographic artifacts, per manufacturer recommendation.
After the recommended conditioning procedure is completed, analyze the IBs and MBs to verify the preconcentrator system meets the method criteria.

Optimize GC conditions for compound separation and sensitivity as indicated by baseline separation for the targeted compounds by establishing GC carrier gas flow rates, oven temperature program, and instrument run time based on the manufacturer’s recommendations and customize, as needed, to separate the desired target oHAPs.

Tune/optimize the MS to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications upon initial installation of the instrument and following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities.

Conduct a BFB tuning check for linear quadrupole or ion trap MS instruments before the ICAL and at least once during every 24-hour period of analyzing samples, blanks, or calibration standards thereafter.

Introduce 1 to 2 ng of BFB into the preconcentrator and analyze the standard using the preconcentrator parameters established and used for the analysis of calibration standards, QC samples, and field samples.

Ensure that the BFB tuning check shows that the GC-MS system meets the mass spectral ion abundance criteria listed in Table 2 in Section 17 of this method for the target compounds before using the system for any analysis.
Ensure the GC-MS system meets the mass spectral ion abundance criteria for the target compounds.
Adjust the tuning of the MS or take corrective actions if the GC-MS system cannot meet the BFB tuning criteria.
Select IS compound(s) to be used for oHAP analysis.
Evaluate the IS upon receipt for the presence of contaminants by analyzing increasing volumes of the IS.
Add the IS through a dedicated non-sample port in the preconcentrator at the same concentration for each injection.
Each IS compound in each sample injection must be within ±2 seconds of the RT for each IS compound in the most recent calibration.
The area response for each IS compound in each injection must be within ± 30% of the mean area response of the IS compound determined from the ICAL.
Choose the quantitation ion for each IS compound as the most abundant ion unless there is a spectral interference.
You must invalidate then reanalyze any samples for which the IS area response differs by more than 30% from the mean IS area response.
Calibrate the GC-MS initially, annually, whenever CCV standards exceed acceptance criteria, or when the system is out of control.
Prior to calibration, analyze a sufficient number of humidified HCF zero air blanks or humidified check standards to verify that instrument sensitivity is stable.
Prepare the calibration curve by preparing standards that bracket the expected concentration levels at the sampling location(s).
You must include at least five levels in the ICAL to approximate concentrations of target oHAPs expected at the deployment location.
Review the sample collection information documented on the field data page and/or COC form(s) for completeness and accuracy.
Compare the canister label with the sample collection data sheet and verify that the canister and sample IDs are correct.
Measure and record the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or transducer.
Flag the results as invalid if the measured canister absolute pressure exceeds the ±3.5 kPa criterion.
Pressurize the canister with diluent gas to a pressure less than or equal to the final pressure of the standard gas canisters.
Measure the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or pressure transducer just prior to dilution and immediately following dilution.

preconditions.text: If you identify exceedances of target compounds in the zero-air challenge, you must take steps to remove the contamination.
If you identify problems, you must consult the instrument.
You must not use a canister for sampling that has not been cleaned and blanked within 30 days of sampling.

constraints.text: Incomplete deactivation of canister interior surfaces may lead to active sites for adsorption or decomposition of VOCs.
Entrained PM deposited in the canister sampling pathway can adsorb VOCs making them unavailable, potentially contaminating subsequent sampling events.
Under certain conditions, the composition of an air sample may change upon its introduction into the canister.
Condensed water within the canister can result in corrosion of the interior surface.
This method does not address all the safety concerns associated with its use.
The flow control flow check is considered valid if within +/- 10% of the reference flow rate.
If you observe an increase in pressure, the sampling device does not qualify as leak-free.
